nativeRange.habitat: Protected reef slopes, overhangs, and lower reef faces with reduced, diffused light and low to moderate water movement; commonly found plating across shaded ledges and deeper reef zones

decorPreferences: stable rockwork base for encrusting or plating growth
horizontal surface, overhanging ledge, or lower to mid rockwork placement
ample open space around the colony (10+ cm clearance from neighbors)

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental target feeding once or twice weekly with small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, baby brine shrimp, or Reef Roids tends to benefit growth and coloration. Oxypora is reported to feed readily; a turkey baster or pipette for targeted delivery is generally advisable. Feeding often occurs at night when sweeper tentacles are extended.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals placed well out of sweeper tentacle reach
avoidWith: corals within sweeper tentacle range (5–10 cm)
other Oxypora colonies without adequate spacing
aggressive LPS such as Galaxea or Hammer placed nearby
high-flow powerheads directed at colony surface

summary: Oxypora is an LPS genus in family Lobophylliidae, sold as 'Chalice Coral' and prized for vivid color morphs like the ORA Easter Egg Chalice. Generally intermediate care; prefers low-to-moderate light and gentle flow.
careNotes:
Chalice trade-umbrella note: 'Chalice Coral' is a broad commercial umbrella name applied to several genera in the aquarium hobby. The true chalice clade — Echinophyllia (see [echinophyllia-coral]) and Oxypora (this entry) — both belong to family Lobophylliidae and are genuinely closely related. Mycedium (see [mycedium-coral]) was historically grouped with this clade but is reclassified to Merulinidae per WoRMS / Budd et al. 2012 molecular revision; despite the shared chalice trade name and plating growth form, it is more closely related to Echinopora than to true chalice. Echinopora (see [echinopora-coral]) is also sold as chalice but belongs to family Merulinidae; the shared trade name reflects convergent plating growth form, not close phylogenetic relationship. Other genera sometimes marketed as chalice include Pectinia and Lithophyllon.

ORA Easter Egg Chalice: The best-known trade strain is the ORA Easter Egg Chalice (Oxypora sp.), characterized by ultra-pink tissue with bright yellow oral disks. ORA obtained the original mother colony from a LiveAquaria display tank at MACNA Orlando 2010 and has propagated it through six or more aquaculture generations. Frags are widely available from reef specialty vendors. The ECC is considered among the more forgiving Oxypora morphs in captivity.

Taxonomy: The genus Oxypora was established by Saville-Kent in 1871. WoRMS currently recognizes five species: O. lacera (Verrill, 1864 — the most commonly encountered in trade), O. crassispinosa, O. convoluta, O. glabra, and O. egyptensis. Most aquarium specimens are sold at genus level (Oxypora sp.) or by color-morph trade name without species identification. The family placement is Lobophylliidae, order Scleractinia (confirmed per WoRMS and Wikipedia). Older sources may list the family as Pectiniidae (pre-2012 taxonomy).

Lighting: Oxypora is generally reported to prefer the lower end of the chalice lighting range. BRS lists 150–250 PAR for the ORA ECC; WWC and ReefChasers converge on 50–100 PAR for chalice genera; cross-source average for this specific genus points to 75–150 PAR as a workable range, with around 100 PAR as a practical sweet spot. This is notably lower than Echinophyllia (which can handle 200 PAR) and significantly lower than SPS. New specimens should be acclimated over 2–3 weeks starting at lower light; bleaching from excess PAR is a known risk.

Flow: Low to moderate flow is generally advised — sufficient to oxygenate tissue and clear debris, but not strong enough to retract polyps or prevent sweeper tentacles from extending for feeding. TankFacts notes that strong flow prevents tentacle extension and reduces feeding efficacy.

Aggression: Oxypora extends sweeper tentacles at night, typically reported at 5–10 cm. Placement must account for full nighttime extension. The 10+ cm clearance recommendation in decorPreferences is a conservative working minimum.

Colony morphology: Colonies are thin-plated (laminar), commonly just 1–4 mm thick except near the attachment point. In the wild, O. lacera rarely exceeds 30–40 cm across (Wikipedia); captive specimens typically reach 10–25 cm before tank constraints limit further expansion. The thin skeleton makes colonies comparatively brittle and porous — handle with care to avoid fracture.

Water quality: Stable parameters are important. Nitrate around 1–20 ppm and phosphate 0.01–0.1 ppm are documented as workable (BRS product page). TankFacts notes susceptibility to brown jelly infection if water conditions are not maintained; stable parameters reduce this risk. Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium supplementation as for other LPS corals is recommended.

Parameter notes — cross-source summary: Temperature 23–27°C averaged from BRS (76–80°F) and TankFacts (74–82°F); kH 8–10 averaged from BRS (8–12) and ReefChasers (8–9); salinity 1.023–1.026 averaged from BRS (1.025–1.026) and TankFacts (1.023–1.025). pH 8.1–8.4 is the two-source consensus (BRS and ReefChasers; TankFacts lower bound of 7.8 treated as an outlier).

Fragging: Relatively straightforward — colonies can be fragged with bone cutters or a band saw. The thin skeleton means smaller frags (2–3 cm) can work, though care is needed to avoid cracking. Mount on frag plugs or rubble with coral glue; frags typically encrust and begin growing within a few weeks under stable conditions. Dipping new frags before introduction is advisable.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity. ORA has aquacultured Oxypora through multiple generations, making captive-bred specimens widely available. O. lacera is documented as a hermaphrodite in the wild, releasing gametes for planktonic larval dispersal; deliberate sexual breeding is not practiced in home aquaria.

primary.name: Bulk Reef Supply – ORA Easter Egg Chalice Coral

additional.name: ReefChasers – Chalice Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked pH (8.0–8.4), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), calcium (430–460 ppm), PAR (50–125 low, 125–250 moderate), and care level (intermediate). Confirmed chalice umbrella genera and aggressive sweeper tentacle character. Temperature range 77–84°F factored into cross-source average.

additional.name: TankFacts – Oxypora crassispinosa Chalice
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (23–27°C), salinity (1.023–1.025), pH (7.8–8.4), alkalinity (8–12 dKH), calcium (380–450 ppm), magnesium (1200–1400 ppm), flow (low to moderate), and aggression. pH lower bound of 7.8 treated as an outlier; two-source consensus of 8.1–8.4 adopted.

additional.name: World Wide Corals – Proper Chalice Coral Care
additional.notes: Confirmed low PAR preference (50–100 PAR) for chalice genera, light to moderate flow, weekly target feeding with meaty foods, and multi-genera umbrella scope (Echinophyllia, Echinopora, Oxypora, Mycedium).

additional.name: ORA – Easter Egg Chalice product page
additional.notes: Confirmed placement (bottom–middle), growth form (thin plating, fast-growing), hardiness, and aquaculture origin history (mother colony from MACNA Orlando 2010 LiveAquaria display). Flow listed as moderate–strong — broader than other sources; mid-range ('medium') adopted as consensus.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Oxypora lacera
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Lobophylliidae, order Scleractinia), native range (Red Sea to Japan, Australia), depth range (~30 m), colony morphology (thin laminar plates, rarely >30 cm), and reproductive mode (hermaphrodite broadcast spawner). Not used for husbandry parameters.
